title: Bilayer crystals in a polar-molecules system
authors: Vinicius Zampronio, Matteo Ciardi, Fabio Cinti
abstract: We investigate the finite-temperature phase diagram of polar molecules confined in a quasi-two-dimensional geometry by a harmonic potential along the polarization axis. We employ Quantum Monte Carlo simulations to explore the strongly correlated regime accessible with current experimental setups. By tuning temperature and confinement strength, we identify a rich set of phases, including normal fluid, superfluid, supersolid, cluster crystal, and bilayer crystal states. Our results reveal the emergence of crystallization upon increasing temperature, highlighting the nontrivial role of thermal fluctuations in dipolar systems. In particular, we show that a bilayer crystal with one molecule per lattice site can be stabilized by varying the confinement strength at fixed interaction. Moreover, we show evidence of layering of superfluid states with phase coherence between the two layers. These findings provide insight into the interplay between interactions, confinement, and temperature in low-dimensional dipolar systems, and suggest new directions for engineering quantum phases with ultracold polar molecules.
summary: This paper uses Path-Integral Monte Carlo simulations to map the phase diagram of polar molecules in a quasi-2D trap. It discovers that heating the system can actually drive crystallization and shows how tuning confinement can stabilize a bilayer crystal state. These findings are crucial for designing future experiments with ultracold polar molecules.
main_problem: Investigating the finite-temperature phase diagram and structural properties of polar molecules in a quasi-two-dimensional geometry under varying confinement and temperature.
main_result: The study identifies a rich phase diagram including normal fluid, superfluid, supersolid, cluster crystal, and bilayer crystal states, notably revealing that increasing temperature can induce crystallization (thermocrystallization).
method: Path-Integral Monte Carlo (PIMC) simulations using the worm algorithm to sample particle permutations.
model_or_system: A system of N bosonic polar molecules (e.g., NaCs or SrO) with long-range dipolar and van der Waals interactions, confined by a harmonic potential along the polarization axis.
key_observables: Structure factor S(k), momentum-space contrast q(k), superfluid fraction f_s, and permutation cycle probability P(L).
important_parameters: Interaction strength D, confinement strength Omega_z, temperature k_B T, and density rho.
main_assumptions: Use of the primitive approximation for the density matrix and discretization of imaginary-time paths into M time slices.
figures_summary: Snapshots of particle worldlines and structure factors for different phases (SS, CC, BC); 1D density profiles along the z-axis; quantitative plots of structure factor contrast, superfluid fraction, and permutation cycles; and comparisons of different interaction strengths.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical system and model, describes the PIMC methodology, presents the identified phase diagram and the phenomenon of thermocrystallization, analyzes the effects of confinement strength on layering and cluster formation, and concludes with experimental implications and future directions.
why_interesting: It explores the non-trivial interplay between long-range interactions, thermal fluctuations, and dimensionality, providing a roadmap for engineering complex quantum phases like supersolids and bilayer crystals in ultracold molecular experiments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Graphic representation of the bilayer state. In blue and red we have a snapshot of the worldlines sampled in the path-integral Monte Carlo simulation (see text). The system is made of molecules polarized along the z axis and confined by a harmonic potential of intensity Ωz in the polarization direction (represented in black).

Figure 2. Phase diagram for the system of polar molecules confined in the z direction by a harmonic potential V (z) = Ωzz2 as a function of Ωz and temperature T. The phase di- agram displays superfluid (SF), supersolid (SS), normal fluid (NF), cluster crystal (CC), and bilayer crystal (BC) states.

Figure 3. (a) Snapshot of the worldlines of the SS state in the y −z plane. (b) A cut of the snapshot of the SS state in the x −y plane showing the percolations; the gray dots represent a particular time slice. (c) The structure factor associated to (b). The physical parameters in (a)-(c) are Ωz = 0.2, kBT = 0.2. (d) A snapshot of the PIMC world lines in the y −z plane, showing the length of the filaments in the CC state. (e) A snapshot in the x −y plane showing the triangular pattern. (f) The structure factor of the triangular pattern in the x −y plane. The physical parameters in (d)- (f) are Ωz = 0.2, kBT = 0.4. (g) Snapshot in the y −z plane showing the separation in two layers. (h)...

Figure 4. Snapshot of particle positions along z versus ρz for different temperatures and confinement strengths (T = 2.0 green line, T = 1.0 light blue line, and T = 0.4 violet line). (a) Weak confinement with Ωz = 0.2. For intermediate confinement (b) Ωz = 20 and (c) Ωz = 200 we observe density modulations that depend on the temperature. (d) Strong confinement regime Ωz = 2000, where the system splits in two layers as the temperature increases. The density of the system is ρ = 2.31 and the interaction strength is D = 35.

Figure 6. Snapshots of the worldlines in the PIMC simulation. In (a)-(c) the interaction strength is D = 127 and in (d)-(f) D = 11. (a) and (d) depicts the three-dimensional system. Projections of the worldlines in the x −y and y −z planes are represented in (b,e) and (c,f), respectively. The harmonic confinement strength is Ωz = 200 and the temperature is kBT = 2.
